justification: All 7 business rules identified with correct invariant classifications. BR-7 (refund window) correctly marked invariant: false with sound reasoning. BR-1 correctly captures that cart-add does NOT reserve inventory — reservation occurs only at checkout. BR-2 (15-min timeout) marked invariant: false, which is debatable but well-reasoned. BR-4 captures discount/promotion mutual exclusivity. Multiple ambiguities surfaced (discount precedence, timer start point, delivery definition for refund window). 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, far exceeding the 4 minimum. Confidence level of 55 is appropriate given the stated assumptions and ambiguities.

observations.text: All 7 rules identified with clear testable assertions for each
Refund window correctly classified as invariant: false with good justification (policy that could change for VIP/holidays)
Cart-add vs checkout reservation distinction explicitly captured in BR-1 and reinforced in ubiquitous language
Discount/promotion mutual exclusivity captured in BR-4 with escalation for unresolved precedence question
12 acceptance criteria — triple the minimum requirement, covering happy paths and boundary cases
Rich edge cases including race conditions (two customers reserving last unit, payment gateway timeout at minute 14)
Ambiguities surfaced: discount precedence strategy, promotional price lock timing, calendar vs business days for refund window

BR-2 (15-minute reservation timeout) marked invariant: false — the rubric expects invariant: true as an inventory management rule. The agent's reasoning is defensible (the duration is policy) but the reservation-release mechanism itself is an integrity constraint
Escalation section provides concrete options (A-D) with recommendation for the discount precedence ambiguity
Business impact analysis is thorough, covering financial, operational, user, and risk dimensions with specific stakeholder mapping
